ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general soy milk entry; no specific flavoured or less-sugar variant exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0EJW covers flavoured soya milk; no less-sugar sub-variant code exists. Closest defensible code used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for flavoured less-sugar packaged soy milk; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single-serve carton of packaged flavoured soy milk in Singapore is 250 ml (approximately 250 g), commonly consumed as a breakfast drink or snack beverage.

nutrient_source.title: Flavoured Soy Milk (Less Sugar) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id D02030001 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per-100g values are taken directly from the HPB record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — soy milk, full-fat (GI 34)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for flavoured less-sugar packaged soy milk. GI 34 is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for full-fat soy milk (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). Flavoured less-sugar soy milk has a comparable carbohydrate profile (6.18 g/100g vs ~4–5 g/100g for plain soy milk); the added sugar (4 g/100g) is modest and the protein and fibre content attenuate glycaemic response, making GI 34 a defensible analogous estimate. GL per 250 g serving = (34 × 6.18 × 2.5) / 100 = 5.25, rounded to 5.2.

hypertension.key_factor: Added sugar in a sweetened beverage context; sodium is low at 21 mg/100g but added sugar can drive insulin-mediated sodium retention
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid sweetened soy milk including 'less sugar' variants and choose unsweetened soy milk (0 g added sugar) to prevent sugar-driven insulin-mediated sodium retention and cardiovascular risk amplification.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Added sugar (4 g/100g) classifies this as a sugar-sweetened beverage; all SSBs including 'less sugar' variants are unsuitable for Type 2 Diabetes management regardless of absolute sugar quantity
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this product as it contains added sugar and is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage; unsweetened soy milk with 0 g added sugar is the recommended alternative to obtain soy protein and isoflavone benefits without glycaemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Soy isoflavones and low saturated fat are cardioprotective, but added sugar in a liquid form raises triglyceride and cardiovascular risk concerns
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While soy protein and isoflavones in soy milk have documented LDL-lowering and cardioprotective properties, the added sugar (4 g/100g) in this flavoured variant can elevate triglycerides and should be replaced with unsweetened soy milk for cardiovascular disease patients.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Soy isoflavones with anti-inflammatory properties; high calcium (166 mg/100g) supports bone protection for patients on corticosteroids
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Soy milk provides isoflavones with documented anti-inflammatory properties and calcium (166 mg/100g) beneficial for bone protection in RA patients on corticosteroids; however, unsweetened soy milk is preferred to avoid sugar-driven pro-inflammatory mediators.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Flavoured Soy Milk (Less Sugar, Packaged) is a ready-to-drink plant-based beverage made from soybeans, water, flavouring agents, and a reduced quantity of added sugar. It is widely available in Singapore and across Southeast Asia in single-serve cartons of approximately 250 ml. The product has a mild, slightly sweet soy flavour with a smooth, thin liquid texture. It is fortified with calcium (166 mg/100g), making it a notable plant-based calcium source. Per 100 g it provides 48 kcal, 2.95 g protein, 1.33 g fat, 6.18 g carbohydrate (of which 4 g is sugar), 1.01 g dietary fibre, and 21 mg sodium. Despite the 'less sugar' label, the product still contains added sugar and is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) for clinical purposes. It is therefore unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or hypertension who require strict sugar control. For cardiovascular health, the soy isoflavones and low saturated fat are beneficial, but the added sugar attenuates this benefit. For rheumatoid arthritis, the isoflavones provide anti-inflammatory support and the calcium content supports bone health. The glycemic index is estimated at 34 (low GI) based on analogous plain soy milk data from the University of Sydney GI Database. The product is vegan, dairy-free, gluten-free, and suitable for halal diets. Unsweetened soy milk is the preferred alternative for all clinical populations requiring sugar restriction.
common_user_queries: Is less sugar soy milk good for diabetics?
Can I drink flavoured soy milk if I have hypertension?
How much sugar is in less sugar soy milk?
What is the GI of soy milk?
Is soy milk good for rheumatoid arthritis?
How much calcium is in soy milk?
Is less sugar soy milk suitable for weight loss?
What is the difference between less sugar and unsweetened soy milk?

confidence_reason: All core nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D02030001, review_status reviewed, confidence 0.95). The GI value is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for plain soy milk, which is the closest approved GI source available. Saturated fat (0.2 g/100g) and trans fat (0.0 g/100g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for flavoured soy milk (USDA SR Legacy #16222) as HPB does not report fatty acid breakdown; this is documented in notes.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (0.2 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for soy milk, flavoured (NDB #16222), as the HPB source does not report fatty acid sub-fractions. Trans fat is confirmed 0.0 g/100g by product category (plant-based, no hydrogenated oils). 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid, micronutrient, and moisture data for this specific flavoured less-sugar packaged soy milk variant are not reported in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central for an exact match as of 2026-04-16. 3. GI of 34 is an analogous estimate based on plain full-fat soy milk from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008); no direct GI measurement for flavoured less-sugar packaged soy milk was found in approved sources. GL per 250 g serving = (34 × 6.18 × 2.5) / 100 = 5.25, rounded to 5.2. 4. Cholesterol is 0.0 g/100g: confirmed zero for plant-based soy milk with no animal-derived ingredients. 5. The 'less sugar' designation does not exempt this product from the SSB classification under clinical dietary guidelines for diabetes and hypertension management.
